parameters.anchor: Definitional: 1 point = one focused person-day of an average performer
parameters.limitation: Averages over role mix; the org sim does not model individual performers.
parameters.plainLanguage: How much focused work one average person can finish in a day, before coordination overhead.

parameters.anchor: Definitional: throughput-5 entity = 1.0 point/step, consistent with orgExecPointsPerPersonPerStep
parameters.limitation: Linear scaling of the 1-10 throughput scale is a simplification.
parameters.plainLanguage: Converts an entity's 1-10 throughput rating into work points per day.

parameters.anchor: Editorial: sized so a healthy 20-person org runs near (not over) capacity
parameters.limitation: Real demand is bursty and seasonal; arrivals here are steady.
parameters.plainLanguage: How much new work shows up per person per day.

parameters.anchor: Editorial elicitation: routine items take 1.5-4 focused person-days
parameters.limitation: Org-specific; adjustable via config.
parameters.plainLanguage: Size of a routine work item: a couple of days.

parameters.anchor: Editorial elicitation
parameters.limitation: Org-specific; adjustable via config.
parameters.plainLanguage: Size of a complex work item: about a week.

parameters.anchor: Editorial elicitation
parameters.limitation: Novel work has the fattest real-world tails; the triangular max understates true worst cases.
parameters.plainLanguage: Size of a novel work item: one to three weeks, uncertain.

parameters.anchor: Editorial: most org work is routine
parameters.limitation: Mix varies enormously by industry and team charter.
parameters.formula: class ~ {routine: taskMixRoutineOrg, complex: taskMixComplexOrg, novel: remainder} (M18)
parameters.plainLanguage: Share of org work that is routine.

parameters.anchor: Editorial
parameters.limitation: Mix varies by org; novel share is the remainder (default 0.15).
parameters.plainLanguage: Share of org work that is complex but not novel.

parameters.anchor: Editorial: at most ~2 people can effectively swarm one task per day
parameters.limitation: Some work parallelizes better than this cap allows.
parameters.formula: per-task allocation cap in the execution loop (Sec 5.1 step 7)
parameters.plainLanguage: How much progress a single task can absorb in one day, no matter how many people are free.

parameters.anchor: Calibrated so a 12-person meeting-heavy flat team pays ~30% coordination tax, directionally consistent with Asana (2024) meeting-load data
parameters.limitation: Not a measured constant; the single most influential editorial coefficient in the org sim.
parameters.formula: tau = min(maxCoordinationTax, mu * channelCostFraction * (C/n) * B(n) * (1 - relief)) (M4)
parameters.plainLanguage: How much of a person's day each open communication channel consumes.

parameters.anchor: Direction from SI Labs (2026) meeting research (~60% of meeting time produces no concrete output); the 1.4-1.6x multiplier is an editorial default
parameters.limitation: The 1.4-1.6x range is an inference from meeting-waste research, not a direct measurement of the multiplier, so it is an editorial default.
parameters.plainLanguage: Meeting-heavy cultures pay about one and a half times the coordination tax of async-first cultures.

parameters.anchor: Boundedness requirement (Sec 6)
parameters.limitation: A cap, not an observation.
parameters.plainLanguage: An org never spends literally 100% of its time coordinating; this is the ceiling.

parameters.anchor: Editorial split informed by Asana/Atlassian meeting-load reports
parameters.limitation: Coordination cost manifests differently across orgs.
parameters.plainLanguage: In meeting-heavy orgs, ~70% of coordination cost shows up as actual meetings.

parameters.anchor: Editorial
parameters.limitation: As above.
parameters.plainLanguage: Async-first orgs take less of their coordination cost as meetings.

parameters.anchor: Editorial: each pair of coupled teams maintains a few active coordination channels
parameters.limitation: Real inter-team coupling depends on the dependency graph, not just pair counts.
parameters.formula: C_inter = interTeamChannelCoefficient * kappa_topology * T(T-1)/2 * (1 + conwayMisalignmentPenalty * m) (M2)
parameters.plainLanguage: How many working channels a typical pair of teams keeps open.

parameters.anchor: Editorial reference level
parameters.limitation: Topology labels compress many real designs.
parameters.plainLanguage: Pod structures keep the standard level of team-pair coupling.

parameters.anchor: Editorial: vertical routing substitutes for some peer-to-peer channels
parameters.limitation: Ignores that vertical routing adds latency (captured separately in M6).
parameters.plainLanguage: Hierarchies route some coordination up-and-down instead of peer-to-peer, cutting direct channels but adding latency.

parameters.anchor: Editorial: federated designs minimize cross-unit coupling by charter
parameters.limitation: Assumes the federation charter is actually respected.
parameters.plainLanguage: Federated structures deliberately decouple units, halving pair coupling.

parameters.anchor: Direction: MacCormack, Rusnak & Baldwin (2012); Nagappan et al. (2008). Magnitude: editorial
parameters.limitation: The mirroring effect is peer-reviewed; this specific gain is not.
parameters.plainLanguage: When ownership and structure are fully misaligned, cross-team coordination needs nearly double the channels.

parameters.anchor: Editorial: ownership ambiguity accretes as teams are added without restructuring
parameters.limitation: Deliberate restructuring (not modeled in v1) resets this in reality.
parameters.plainLanguage: Every team added without restructuring makes who-owns-what a bit murkier.

parameters.anchor: Editorial: delegated ownership absorbs some ambiguity
parameters.limitation: Federation quality varies.
parameters.plainLanguage: Federated charters soak up some of the ownership ambiguity that growth creates.

parameters.anchor: Editorial: share of decisions that span team boundaries
parameters.limitation: Depends on architecture modularity (Conway again).
parameters.plainLanguage: Roughly a third of decisions touch more than one team - those are the ones that escalate when ownership is unclear.

parameters.anchor: Editorial, consistent with SI Labs multi-layer delay observations
parameters.limitation: Escalation cost varies with executive attention, not modeled.
parameters.formula: service += Triangular(escalationExtraDays) on escalated entry to layer L (M6)
parameters.plainLanguage: A decision nobody clearly owns waits about a week extra while it gets escalated.

parameters.anchor: Dunbar (1992) support-clique tier; used as a planning heuristic per Atlassian/CTO-practice frameworks
parameters.limitation: Contested: Lind et al. (2021) find the underlying method imprecise; treated as an adjustable band, not a law.
parameters.plainLanguage: Around five people, everyone shares full context effortlessly.

parameters.anchor: Dunbar sympathy-group tier; industry planning heuristic
parameters.limitation: As cognitiveBandInner.
parameters.formula: band center in B(n) (M3); also the cohesion size penalty midpoint (M12)
parameters.plainLanguage: Past ~15 people, a team stops being one trusted unit.

parameters.anchor: Dunbar band tier; industry planning heuristic
parameters.limitation: As cognitiveBandInner.
parameters.plainLanguage: Past ~50, working relationships need explicit structure.

parameters.anchor: Dunbar's 150; range extends to ~290 per the alternative US estimate (McCarty et al.)
parameters.limitation: The most contested number in the model; the range deliberately spans the critique.
parameters.plainLanguage: Somewhere between 100 and 300 people, nobody knows everyone anymore.

parameters.anchor: Editorial
parameters.limitation: Penalty magnitudes are tuned, not measured.
parameters.plainLanguage: Crossing the ~5-person band adds a small coordination surcharge.

parameters.anchor: Editorial
parameters.limitation: As bandPenaltyInner.
parameters.plainLanguage: Crossing ~15 adds a moderate surcharge.

parameters.anchor: Editorial
parameters.limitation: As bandPenaltyInner.
parameters.plainLanguage: Crossing ~50 adds a bigger surcharge.

parameters.anchor: Editorial
parameters.limitation: As bandPenaltyInner.
parameters.plainLanguage: Crossing ~150 adds the largest surcharge.

parameters.anchor: Editorial: makes band crossings gradual, per the design red-team requirement (no cliffs)
parameters.limitation: Width is a smoothing choice, not an observation.
parameters.plainLanguage: How gradual each band crossing feels - the model never has a cliff at exactly 150.

parameters.anchor: eigenorg editorial default derived from the SI Labs hierarchy-latency pattern
parameters.limitation: Context-dependent; user-adjustable by design.
parameters.plainLanguage: How much each extra ownership layer drags on the decision-velocity score.

parameters.anchor: SI Labs (2026): ~3 days average processing per approval layer; design default 2-3 days
parameters.limitation: Documented mainly in large hierarchical orgs; flat orgs and startups differ (Flat Paradox).
parameters.plainLanguage: Each approval layer sits on a decision for two to three working days.

parameters.anchor: Editorial: a first-line decision layer processes ~6 items/day
parameters.limitation: Real capacity depends on decision size and delegation.
parameters.plainLanguage: How many decisions the first layer can actually process per day.

parameters.anchor: Editorial: senior layers review fewer items per day
parameters.limitation: Some execs batch-approve; decay is not universal.
parameters.plainLanguage: Every layer up the stack has ~30% less decision bandwidth - which is why queues form at the top.

parameters.anchor: Editorial
parameters.limitation: Override frequency data is scarce; tuned so ~10-15% of tasks in a 3-layer stack get overridden.
parameters.plainLanguage: The per-day chance that a higher layer reverses an in-flight piece of work, per extra layer above it.

parameters.anchor: Editorial
parameters.limitation: Tuned, not measured.
parameters.plainLanguage: Unclear ownership makes overrides much more likely - people relitigate decisions nobody clearly owned.

parameters.anchor: design red-team fix: overridden tasks re-enter at 50% completion (partial rework), not 0%
parameters.limitation: Real rework cost depends on how contested the direction was.
parameters.plainLanguage: An overridden task keeps about half its progress - rework, not restart.

parameters.anchor: Editorial: information degrades at each human relay
parameters.limitation: Distortion research (serial reproduction) is qualitative; the number is editorial.
parameters.formula: distortion = distortionPerHumanLayer * (D - 1); feeds override probability (M8) and perLayer stats
parameters.plainLanguage: Every human-to-human relay of context loses about 8% of the signal.

parameters.anchor: Editorial
parameters.limitation: Tuned, not measured; couples two editorial quantities.
parameters.plainLanguage: The more the story got garbled on its way up, the more likely the top layer overrides the call.

parameters.anchor: Standard exponential smoothing; editorial constant
parameters.limitation: Affects chart smoothness and entropy responsiveness, not steady-state values.
parameters.plainLanguage: How quickly the displayed latency and entropy react to what just happened versus the recent past.

parameters.anchor: Editorial: share of routine work AI agents actually touch after an org-level injection
parameters.limitation: Adoption is uneven in reality.
parameters.formula: routine allocations boosted on this share (M11); effectiveAiShare for cohesion (M12)
parameters.plainLanguage: When an org 'adds AI', about 60% of routine work actually flows through the agents.

parameters.anchor: Editorial, conservative relative to vendor claims
parameters.limitation: Deliberately conservative; vendor-reported gains are usually task-level, not org-level.
parameters.formula: routine execution allocation *= (1 + aiRoutineShareOrg * (aiThroughputBoostOrg - 1)) when AI active - uniform expected-value factor, no per-task share draw (M11(a))
parameters.plainLanguage: AI makes the routine work it touches about 25% faster at org level.

parameters.anchor: Editorial: AI routing/summarizing relieves coordination in structurally healthy orgs
parameters.limitation: Relief assumes agents are wired into clear ownership - which is exactly what low-SH orgs lack. Range extends above the default so calibration has upward headroom.
parameters.plainLanguage: In a healthy org, AI trims up to about a third of the coordination tax. In an unhealthy one: nothing.

parameters.anchor: HatchWorks (2025) AI-agent failure-mode analysis; editorial calibration
parameters.limitation: Agent failure rates vary by domain and tooling generation; adjustable by design.
parameters.plainLanguage: Roughly one in five novel tasks routed through an AI agent without human judgment hits a failure it cannot handle.

parameters.anchor: Editorial baseline
parameters.limitation: Humans fail on novel work too - just far less often and more recoverably.
parameters.formula: brittleness probability when a human covers the judgment path (M11)
parameters.plainLanguage: Humans stumble on a few percent of novel tasks.

parameters.anchor: design red-team range 1.3-1.8x, synthesized from AI-transformation failure reports (rework.com 2025, aimagicx 2026)
parameters.limitation: Practitioner-report-based, not a controlled study; the model's most load-bearing editorial coefficient.
parameters.plainLanguage: In a structurally unhealthy org, AI injection multiplies novel-task failures by about 1.5x - faster dysfunction.

parameters.anchor: Editorial: healthy orgs keep humans in the loop, catching most agent failures
parameters.limitation: Assumes guardrails actually exist at high SH.
parameters.plainLanguage: In a healthy org, most AI stumbles get caught before they become failures.

parameters.anchor: design red-team: 'when Structural Health is low (<= 4)'
parameters.limitation: The 1-10 SH scale is itself a diagnostic heuristic (Sec 3.4).
parameters.formula: amplification is full at SH <= shRiskThreshold (M9)
parameters.plainLanguage: At Structural Health 4 or below, AI amplifies dysfunction at full strength.

parameters.anchor: Editorial
parameters.limitation: As shRiskThreshold.
parameters.formula: guardrailed regime at SH >= shSafeThreshold; linear in between (M9)
parameters.plainLanguage: At Structural Health 7 or above, AI injection actually helps.

parameters.anchor: Editorial: SH question 5 (recovery path) dominates this
parameters.limitation: Org sim derives ownership from SH; team sim uses the explicit recoveryOwner config.
parameters.formula: org sim: recovery is owned iff SH >= recoveryOwnershipThreshold (M10)
parameters.plainLanguage: Below Structural Health 5, nobody clearly owns failure recovery.

parameters.anchor: design red-team fix: 1.5-2.5x latency for 3-5 steps when no recovery owner
parameters.limitation: Range is an editorial synthesis.
parameters.plainLanguage: An unowned failure roughly doubles decision times while people figure out whose problem it is.

parameters.anchor: design red-team fix: next 3-5 time steps
parameters.limitation: As recoveryLatencyMultiplierUnowned.
parameters.plainLanguage: The unowned-failure slowdown lasts most of a week.

parameters.anchor: Editorial
parameters.limitation: Assumes the named owner is competent and available.
parameters.plainLanguage: With a named recovery owner, a failure barely dents decision speed.

parameters.anchor: Editorial
parameters.limitation: As recoveryLatencyMultiplierOwned.
parameters.plainLanguage: Owned recoveries wrap up in a day or two.

parameters.anchor: Stanford/Carnegie study as reported by e-discoveryteam.com (2025): hybrid teams beat fully autonomous agent teams by 68.7% on complex tasks
parameters.limitation: Lab-based, domain-specific (law, drone design, creative tasks), secondhand-reported; treated as an adjustable editorial default and deliberately NOT presented as a universal constant. For routine tasks the advantage may reverse.
parameters.formula: judgment contribution /= hybridVsAutonomousAdvantage for complex/novel tasks with zero human judgment coverage (M16)
parameters.plainLanguage: On complex work, teams that keep humans in the judgment loop outperform all-AI pipelines by a large margin - about 69% in the one study we anchor to, which is why we let you adjust it.

parameters.anchor: Editorial, consistent with the hybrid-team literature's routine-task findings
parameters.limitation: Task-type dependent.
parameters.plainLanguage: AI executes routine work about 50% faster than humans.

parameters.anchor: Editorial: agents triage in minutes-to-hours, humans in days
parameters.limitation: Assumes the routing genuinely is routine.
parameters.formula: routine prioritization service *= aiRoutineLatencyFactor when AI covers prioritization (M11)
parameters.plainLanguage: An AI prioritization agent turns a 2.5-day routine triage into a same-day one.

parameters.anchor: Editorial: capability collapse on novel work
parameters.limitation: Improving frontier; adjustable.
parameters.plainLanguage: On novel work an AI executor delivers less than a third of its routine effectiveness.

parameters.anchor: Editorial: unowned queues self-organize slowly
parameters.limitation: Small teams sometimes self-prioritize well.
parameters.formula: prioritization service *= uncoveredPrioritizationFactor when nobody covers prioritization (M11)
parameters.plainLanguage: If nobody owns prioritization, everything waits 50% longer to get picked up.

parameters.anchor: Editorial
parameters.limitation: Stakes and novelty correlate in reality; modeled independently here.
parameters.formula: brittleness probability *= highStakesBrittlenessFactor for high-stakes novel tasks (M9, team sim)
parameters.plainLanguage: High-stakes novel work is where AI failures concentrate.

parameters.anchor: Editorial baseline for a functioning team
parameters.limitation: Starting cohesion varies; the dynamics matter more than the level.
parameters.formula: cohesion target ceiling and initial value (M12)
parameters.plainLanguage: A normal healthy team sits around 75/100 on cohesion.

parameters.anchor: Editorial, keyed to the ~15-person band
parameters.limitation: Band location is contested (Sec 9.3).
parameters.plainLanguage: Teams that outgrow ~15 people lose about a dozen points of glue.

parameters.anchor: Direction from WEF (2026) and AMCIS 2025 (AI-heavy teams report weaker co-worker connections); magnitude is an editorial default
parameters.limitation: The direction is survey-supported but the magnitude is scaled editorially to the 0-100 index, so this is an editorial default, not a reported figure.
parameters.plainLanguage: The more teammates are agents, the weaker the human bonds get - about 15 points if the whole team were AI.

parameters.anchor: Editorial, modeling the Hollow Middle red-team finding (front-line teams lose the human relationship layer)
parameters.limitation: Binary trigger; real hollowing is gradual.
parameters.formula: target -= cohesionHollownessPenalty when prioritization, coordination, or stakeholderCommunication has zero human coverage (M12)
parameters.plainLanguage: Replacing the human relationship layer (like the PM people actually talked to) costs the team extra glue.

parameters.anchor: Editorial
parameters.limitation: Recovery speed varies by team history.
parameters.plainLanguage: Cohesion drifts about 5% of the way toward its ceiling (or floor) each day.

parameters.anchor: Editorial (the L3 reinforcing loop, Sec 6)
parameters.limitation: Bounded by clamps; see Sec 6 L3 for the stability argument.
parameters.plainLanguage: Sustained thrash grinds team spirit down a little every day.

parameters.anchor: Editorial
parameters.limitation: Threshold form is a simplification of a continuous effect.
parameters.formula: cohesion coupling activates above this entropy (M12)
parameters.plainLanguage: Below ~60 entropy, people cope; above it, the thrash starts eating the team.

parameters.anchor: Editorial; weights sum to 1 (extractor-checked)
parameters.limitation: Weighting is an editorial judgment about salience, not a fit.
parameters.plainLanguage: Coordination overhead is the biggest single ingredient of entropy.

parameters.anchor: Editorial
parameters.limitation: As entropyWeightCoordination.
parameters.plainLanguage: Slow decisions are the second-biggest ingredient.

parameters.anchor: Editorial
parameters.limitation: As entropyWeightCoordination.
parameters.plainLanguage: Frayed human fabric contributes a fifth.

parameters.anchor: Editorial
parameters.limitation: As entropyWeightCoordination.
parameters.plainLanguage: Failures on novel work contribute when AI is in play.

parameters.anchor: Editorial
parameters.limitation: As entropyWeightCoordination.
parameters.plainLanguage: Piled-up work-in-progress rounds out the index.

parameters.anchor: Editorial: ~2.5 working weeks of decision latency saturates the entropy term
parameters.limitation: Normalization constant, affects entropy scale only.
parameters.plainLanguage: Decisions taking 12+ days count as maximally slow.

parameters.anchor: Editorial
parameters.limitation: Normalization constant.
parameters.plainLanguage: A failure every other day counts as maximal brittleness.

parameters.anchor: Editorial, informed by common WIP-limit practice (1-3 items/person)
parameters.limitation: Normalization constant.
parameters.plainLanguage: More than ~2.5 items in flight per person counts as maximal WIP overload.

parameters.anchor: Editorial
parameters.limitation: Pairwise friction is averaged (M14).
parameters.plainLanguage: A team of average-friction entities loses ~3% of capacity to handoffs.

parameters.anchor: Editorial: humans cover ~3 functions before attention dilutes
parameters.limitation: Individual variance is large.
parameters.formula: attention_e = min(1, humanAttentionSpan / count(functions_e)) for humans (M17)
parameters.plainLanguage: A person juggling more than three functions covers each one thinner; software does not have this problem.

parameters.anchor: Editorial: execution needs several dedicated people
parameters.limitation: Linear team-size scaling is a simplification.
parameters.plainLanguage: Execution is the hungriest function - it needs multiple people's full attention.

parameters.anchor: Editorial
parameters.limitation: As functionDemandExecution.
parameters.plainLanguage: Each judgment/communication function needs about half a dedicated person on an 8-person team.

parameters.anchor: Editorial display threshold
parameters.limitation: Display semantics only; no dynamics hang off this value.
parameters.plainLanguage: 80%+ covered shows green.

parameters.anchor: Editorial display threshold
parameters.limitation: As coverageGreenThreshold.
parameters.formula: rating amber iff coverage >= coverageAmberThreshold (else red) (M17)
parameters.plainLanguage: Below 50% covered shows red - effectively unowned.

parameters.anchor: Editorial
parameters.limitation: Quality index is internally comparable only (M16).
parameters.formula: mu_q = qualityBase + judgment and reliability terms (M16)
parameters.plainLanguage: Work with zero applied judgment starts from a mediocre baseline.

parameters.anchor: Editorial
parameters.limitation: As qualityBase.
parameters.plainLanguage: Strong judgment coverage lifts quality from mediocre to excellent.

parameters.anchor: Editorial
parameters.limitation: As qualityBase.
parameters.plainLanguage: Unreliable builders drag quality down a bit even with good review.

parameters.anchor: Editorial, consistent with the hybrid-team literature's novel-task findings
parameters.limitation: As qualityBase.
parameters.formula: mu_q -= noveltyQualityPenalty for novel tasks with zero human judgment coverage (M16)
parameters.plainLanguage: Novel work shipped without any human judgment takes a big quality hit.

parameters.anchor: Editorial
parameters.limitation: As qualityBase.
parameters.formula: mu_q -= reviewUncoveredQualityPenalty when review is uncovered (M16)
parameters.plainLanguage: Skipping review costs several points of quality on everything.

parameters.anchor: Editorial
parameters.limitation: Symmetric-ish triangular spread is a simplification.
parameters.formula: quality ~ Triangular(mu_q - qualitySpreadDown, mu_q, mu_q + qualitySpreadUp) (M16)
parameters.plainLanguage: Bad days are worse than good days are good - the downside spread is wider.

parameters.anchor: Editorial
parameters.limitation: As qualitySpreadDown.
parameters.plainLanguage: Upside surprise on quality is modest.

parameters.anchor: Editorial
parameters.limitation: Fixed dwell; review queues are not modeled in v1.
parameters.formula: completed tasks enter the review dwell at execution (§5.2 step 6, T6r) and wait reviewDwellDays before clearing to done at review clearance (§5.2 step 7, T6d/M20) when review is covered
parameters.plainLanguage: Review adds about a day before work counts as shipped.

parameters.anchor: Brooks (1975) onboarding cost, magnitude editorial
parameters.limitation: Linear ramp (M15).
parameters.plainLanguage: A new hire starts at about 30% effectiveness.

parameters.anchor: Editorial
parameters.limitation: As qualityBase.
parameters.formula: judgmentEff = judgmentFloor when nobody covers review/synthesis/ambiguity (M16)
parameters.plainLanguage: Even with nobody on judgment functions, some residual judgment happens in the doing.

parameters.anchor: Industry pod-design practice (6-8 person pods)
parameters.limitation: Partition arithmetic, not a behavioral coefficient.
parameters.plainLanguage: Pods are sized around 8 people.

parameters.anchor: Common span-of-control practice (~7 direct reports)
parameters.limitation: As teamPodTargetSize.
parameters.plainLanguage: Hierarchies fan out at about seven people per manager.

parameters.anchor: Editorial: a senior/exec approval seat deliberates longer per item than a first-line PM
parameters.limitation: Seat behavior varies; a decisive director can be faster than a committee-run PM function.
parameters.formula: service draw at a humanDirector layer *= layerLatencyFactorDirector (M6, §9.9)
parameters.plainLanguage: A director seat sits on each decision about 20% longer than a baseline PM seat.

parameters.anchor: Editorial: senior seats review fewer items per day (span-of-attention), amplifying the top-of-stack queue
parameters.limitation: Some directors batch-approve; the reduction is an editorial default.
parameters.formula: cap_l at a humanDirector layer *= layerCapacityFactorDirector (M6, §9.9)
parameters.plainLanguage: A director seat processes about a quarter fewer decisions per day than a baseline PM seat.

parameters.anchor: Editorial: an AI prioritization agent clears a routing decision in a fraction of a human seat's time (consistent with aiRoutineLatencyFactor)
parameters.limitation: Assumes the routing genuinely is mechanical; the agent is no faster on genuinely ambiguous calls.
parameters.formula: service draw at an aiAgent layer *= layerLatencyFactorAiAgent (M6, §9.9)
parameters.plainLanguage: An AI agent seat clears approvals at roughly 40% of a human seat's time.

parameters.anchor: Editorial: an agent adds mechanical approval bandwidth at any Structural Health (M11(c) rationale, applied per-seat)
parameters.limitation: Bandwidth is a modeling proxy; a badly-integrated agent can add rework instead.
parameters.formula: cap_l at an aiAgent layer *= layerCapacityFactorAiAgent (M6, §9.9)
parameters.plainLanguage: An AI agent seat processes about 40% more decisions per day than a baseline PM seat.

parameters.anchor: Editorial: an agent relays context verbatim, so its layer garbles far less than a human hand-up
parameters.limitation: Assumes the agent preserves nuance; over-summarizing agents can distort in their own way.
parameters.plainLanguage: An AI agent seat loses only about a third of the context a human relay would - so it triggers fewer overrides.

parameters.anchor: Editorial: an AI-owned prioritization seat routes novel work into the brittleness path much like an org-wide injection, scaled below 1 because a single seat is narrower than full injection
parameters.limitation: The single load-bearing coefficient that makes an AI middle layer riskier on novel work; deliberately adjustable.
parameters.formula: novelExposure = max(injectionActive?1:0, max_l layerNovelExposure(type_l)); P(brittle) = aiNovelFailureBase * shBrittleFactor(SH) * novelExposure (M11/M9, §9.9)
parameters.plainLanguage: Put an AI agent in an approval seat and about 70% of the AI novel-failure exposure of a full org-wide injection shows up - the routine speed-up comes bundled with novel brittleness.

parameters.anchor: Editorial: committee decision-making is slow (scheduling, consensus)
parameters.limitation: A well-run committee with a decider can beat this; the default models the common slow case.
parameters.formula: service draw at a committee layer *= layerLatencyFactorCommittee (M6, §9.9)
parameters.plainLanguage: A committee seat sits on each decision about 50% longer than a baseline PM seat.

parameters.anchor: Editorial: a committee meets periodically, so its per-day approval bandwidth is low
parameters.limitation: Standing committees with delegated sub-decisions can process more.
parameters.formula: cap_l at a committee layer *= layerCapacityFactorCommittee (M6, §9.9)
parameters.plainLanguage: A committee seat processes about 40% fewer decisions per day than a baseline PM seat.

parameters.anchor: Editorial: multiple reviewers catch and correct relayed context, halving distortion versus a single human hand-up
parameters.limitation: Groupthink can defeat the many-eyes benefit; editorial default.
parameters.plainLanguage: A committee seat's many eyes cut relayed-context loss roughly in half - fewer overrides than a lone human seat.

parameters.unit: override-probability gain per added co-equal owner
parameters.anchor: Darley & Latane (1968, JPSP 8:377-383): decisive helping fell 85%->62%->31% for 1/2/5 responsible parties - the first added co-equal owner cut decisive ownership to ~73% of solo; Latane (1981, American Psychologist 36:343-356) Social Impact Theory fits felt-responsibility ~ N^-0.5. Default 0.4 = a x1.4 override multiplier at the first added owner (mu=2), matching that first-owner drop.
parameters.limitation: The felt-responsibility -> relitigation link is an author construct; no study measures decision-override rates directly, so despite the peer-reviewed diffusion anchor the coefficient is editorial. The linear (mu-1) form overstates diffusion beyond ~4 co-equal owners, where the data saturate (~N^0.5); a cap/power-law is a deferred refinement.
parameters.formula: diffusionFactor_l = 1 + overrideDiffusionGain * (mu_l - 1) * (1 - tiebreaker_l); diffusionMean = mean over l in 2..=L of diffusionFactor_l feeds o(t) (M8/M19)
parameters.plainLanguage: Each extra co-equal owner on one decision makes it about 40% more likely to be relitigated or overridden - and the first added owner is the costliest.

parameters.unit: service-time gain per added co-equal owner
parameters.anchor: Darley & Latane (1968) found intervention latency rose monotonically with bystander count (interventions were fewer AND slower); McKinsey ('Revisiting the matrix organization,' 2016; 'Decision making in the age of urgency,' 2019) reports distributed accountability decides materially slower than single-point accountability. Default +35% per added owner matches the 2-4 owner regime.
parameters.limitation: Direction is well supported; the exact percentage is an anchored estimate, not a measured decision-cycle dataset. Modeled linear in (mu-1); the convex Brooks pairwise-channel form (n(n-1)/2) for >4 co-equal owners is a deferred refinement, kept distinct from the motivation-loss channel per Latane, Williams & Harkins (1979).
parameters.formula: diffusionLatencyFactor_l = 1 + muLatencySurchargeRate * (mu_l - 1) * (1 - tiebreaker_l); multiplies the M6 per-layer service draw at every seat l in 1..L (M19)
parameters.plainLanguage: Each extra co-equal owner adds about 35% to the time a decision sits at that seat - consultation and relitigation rounds pile up.

parameters.unit: extra progress fraction dropped per added co-equal owner
parameters.anchor: Karau & Williams (1993, JPSP 65:681-706) social-loafing meta-analysis (78 studies): motivation-loss d ~= -0.44, ~9-18% per-owner effort loss; Latane, Williams & Harkins (1979, JPSP 37:822-832) isolate motivation loss from coordination loss. Default 0.03 is a deliberately small extra loss, distinct from the latency (coordination) channel.
parameters.limitation: Loafing is measured on additive output tasks, not decision ownership; kept small and applied only at the override event (T7) to avoid double-counting the coordination-latency channel. Set to 0 to disable the motivation-loss channel; identifiability (a named owner) also removes it (Williams, Harkins & Latane 1981).
parameters.formula: on override (T7): progress *= wipResetFraction * max(0, 1 - dropMean), dropMean = mean over l in 2..=L of muWorkDropFraction * (mu_l - 1) * (1 - tiebreaker_l) (M19)
parameters.plainLanguage: A little extra work falls between co-equal owners: when a diffuse decision is overridden it keeps slightly less of its progress than a single-owner one.

parameters.unit: attribution weight per layer above layer 1
parameters.anchor: Authority-gradient / HiPPO effect: steeper authority gradients concentrate reversal power at the top (crew-resource-management authority-gradient research, Fischer & Orasanu 2000; 'HiPPO' - highest-paid-person's-opinion overriding lower-level calls, popularized in evidence-based-management practice). Directional; magnitude editorial. Default 0 = uniform attribution, identical to the v1.0.0 draw - the gradient is exposed as an opt-in lever.
parameters.limitation: Reweights only WHICH seat an override is credited to for the perLayer.overrideShare output; it does not change the override rate, the RNG draw count/order (still exactly one uniform per event), the WIP reset, or any series - so no golden is affected at any value. Default 0.0 keeps the uniform draw, so perLayer.overrideShare is byte-identical to v1 (calibration-safety). A maintainer may elect a default of 0.5 (the HiPPO tilt) only after confirming that no www/presets/*.json Rust plausibility test snapshots perLayer.overrideShare (none do in v1). The gradient's slope is an editorial default; real reversal authority is lumpy, not a smooth linear ramp.
parameters.formula: override event attributed to seat l in {2..L} with probability proportional to w_l = 1 + overrideAuthorityGradient * (l - 1) (M8/M19); one uniform per event
parameters.plainLanguage: Off by default: an override is credited to a uniformly-random higher seat, exactly as v1. Turn it up and the higher seats absorb more of the credit - the boss overturns more calls than the layer just above the work (HiPPO).
